methodology: PubMed, Google Scholar, regulatory databases, WADA prohibited lists, FDA databases

validatedBenefits.claim: Reduces anxiety without sedation or cognitive impairment
validatedBenefits.citation: Zozulya et al., Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova, 2008
validatedBenefits.notes: Clinical study of 62 patients with GAD/neurasthenia. Selank (n=30) showed anxiolytic effects comparable to medazepam without sedation. Also had psychostimulant and antiasthenic effects. Single Russian-language study; not replicated in Western trials.

validatedBenefits.claim: Improves emotional stability and stress resilience
validatedBenefits.citation: Zozulya et al., Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova, 2008
validatedBenefits.notes: Same GAD study showed improved enkephalin activity correlated with reduced anxiety. Functional connectomic study (52 healthy participants) showed effects on amygdala and prefrontal cortex connectivity.

validatedBenefits.citation: Uchakina et al., Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova, 2008
validatedBenefits.notes: Study showed immunomodulatory effects in patients with anxiety-asthenic disorders. Selank is a tuftsin analog, and tuftsin is a known immunomodulatory peptide. Evidence is limited to Russian-language publications.

validatedBenefits.citation: Seredenin et al., Bull Exp Biol Med, 2001
validatedBenefits.notes: Mechanism described via enkephalin-degrading enzyme inhibition. Nootropic claims mostly from animal data and the Russian clinical use context. No large Western RCTs on cognitive endpoints.

validatedBenefits.citation: Kasian et al., Front Pharmacol, 2017
validatedBenefits.notes: In vitro study (IMR-32 cells) showed Selank affects expression of 45 genes involved in GABAergic neurotransmission. Acts as positive allosteric modulator of GABAA receptors. Also influences serotonin, dopamine, and noradrenaline metabolism.
No published human clinical trial data specifically on withdrawal support found in PubMed. This claim appears anecdotal.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Clinical study reported no significant adverse effects. However, dedicated English-language safety studies are absent.
Fatigue, headache, and dizziness mentioned in clinical literature but not frequently enough to establish as a defined side effect.
Commonly reported with intranasal peptide administration. Expected with nasal spray delivery.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 750 mcg-1000 mcg/day (standard Selank); 300-600 mcg/day (N-Acetyl Selank Amidate), administered in 2-3 divided intranasal doses
dosingValidation.citation: Russian prescribing guidelines and clinical protocols
dosingValidation.notes: Our stated range of 250-500 mcg/day is on the LOW side. Published Russian clinical protocols and multiple sources indicate 750-1000 mcg/day for standard Selank. However, some sources do cite lower starting doses. Recommend updating to 250-1000 mcg/day to cover the full range.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: While there are published clinical studies, they are almost exclusively Russian-language, small sample sizes (n=30-52), not replicated in Western trials, and the FDA has flagged immunogenicity concerns. 'Moderate' overstates the evidence quality for a Western audience.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved, removed from Category 2 Sept 2024, registered in Russia. WADA status should be clarified: while not explicitly listed by name, Selank falls under S0 (Non-Approved Substances) on the WADA Prohibited List, making it effectively banned for athletes.

keyCitations.title: Efficacy and possible mechanisms of action of a new peptide anxiolytic selank in the therapy of generalized anxiety disorders and neurasthenia
keyCitations.journal: Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova

keyCitations.title: Selank Administration Affects the Expression of Some Genes Involved in GABAergic Neurotransmission
GABA, Selank, and Olanzapine Affect the Expression of Genes Involved in GABAergic Neurotransmission in IMR-32 Cells
Immunomodulatory effects of selank in patients with anxiety-asthenic disorders
keyCitations.journal: Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova

keyCitations.title: Functional Connectomic Approach to Studying Selank and Semax Effects

corrections: Dosing range 250-500 mcg/day is lower than most published protocols (750-1000 mcg/day for standard Selank). Consider updating to 250-1000 mcg/day.
WADA status should note that Selank is effectively banned under S0 (Non-Approved Substances) even though not listed by name. Our wadaBanned: false is potentially misleading for athletes.
Evidence level should be low_moderate rather than moderate given the lack of Western RCTs.
missingInfo: FDA immunogenicity warning for compounded Selank products should be mentioned
The comparison study vs phenazepam (PMID: 25176261) showing comparable anxiolytic efficacy
The functional connectivity fMRI study in 52 healthy participants showing amygdala/prefrontal effects

validatedBenefits.citation: Aseev et al., Bull Exp Biol Med, 2007; Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation report
validatedBenefits.notes: In a human volunteer study, intranasal Semax sharpened attention and short-term memory, most noticeably under fatigue. In one pilot study of 24 healthy subjects, intranasal 1% semax (1.2 mg total) increased resting fMRI signal in the default mode network. Evidence is limited but supportive.

validatedBenefits.citation: Dolotov et al., Brain Res, 2006
validatedBenefits.notes: Demonstrated in rats: single application caused 1.4-fold BDNF protein increase, 3-fold exon III BDNF mRNA increase in hippocampus. In humans (stroke patients), semax increased plasma BDNF levels (PMID: 29798983). Well-supported mechanism.

validatedBenefits.citation: Eremin et al., Neurochem Res, 2005
validatedBenefits.notes: Demonstrated in rodents: Semax activates dopaminergic and serotoninergic brain systems. Mechanism well-described in animal models.

validatedBenefits.citation: Gusev et al., Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova, 2018
validatedBenefits.notes: Study of 110 ischemic stroke patients. Two 10-day courses of 6000 mcg/day semax increased BDNF levels and improved motor performance and functional independence (Barthel index). This is the strongest human evidence for Semax.
No published clinical trial data on ADHD specifically found. This claim appears extrapolated from dopamine modulation data and anecdotal reports.

validatedBenefits.citation: Multiple preclinical studies
validatedBenefits.notes: Supported by BDNF upregulation mechanism and neurotrophic factor activation data, primarily from animal studies. The stroke recovery data in humans indirectly supports this.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Consistent across Russian clinical use and limited Western research. Low incidence of side effects.
Expected with intranasal delivery. Commonly reported.
Mild and transient. Reported in multiple clinical summaries.
Restlessness and irritability reported with higher doses. Consistent with dopaminergic activation mechanism.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 100-300 mcg/dose, 1-3 times daily for nootropic use (0.1% solution = 2 drops per nostril); 6000 mcg/day (1% solution) for stroke recovery
dosingValidation.citation: Russian prescribing guidelines; Gusev et al., 2018 (PMID: 29798983)
dosingValidation.notes: Our range of 200-600 mcg/day aligns with nootropic dosing. Correctly distinguishes 0.1% (nootropic) from 1% (neurological conditions).

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: The 110-patient stroke study provides meaningful human evidence. Decades of Russian clinical use. BDNF mechanism well-characterized. However, no Western RCTs for nootropic claims. 'Moderate' is appropriate.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved, registered nootropic in Russia, research chemical in US. Like Selank, Semax falls under WADA S0 (Non-Approved Substances). Our wadaBanned: false could be misleading for athletes.

keyCitations.title: Semax, an analog of ACTH(4-10) with cognitive effects, regulates BDNF and trkB expression in the rat hippocampus
The efficacy of semax in the treatment of patients at different stages of ischemic stroke
keyCitations.journal: Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr Im S S Korsakova

keyCitations.title: Semax, an ACTH(4-10) analogue with nootropic properties, activates dopaminergic and serotoninergic brain systems in rodents
A nootropic adrenocorticotropin analog 4-10-semax (15 years experience in its design and study)
keyCitations.journal: Zh Vyssh Nerv Deiat Im I P Pavlova

keyCitations.title: Semax and Pro-Gly-Pro Activate the Transcription of Neurotrophins and Their Receptor Genes after Cerebral Ischemia

corrections: WADA status should note that Semax falls under S0 (Non-Approved Substances) even though wadaBanned is set to false. Potentially misleading for athletes.
ADHD benefit claim has no published clinical evidence and should be flagged as speculative/anecdotal.
missingInfo: The 110-patient stroke study (PMID: 29798983) is the strongest human evidence and should be cited in evidenceNotes
The 24-subject fMRI study showing default mode network effects in healthy subjects
The 15+ year track record as a registered pharmaceutical in Russia (registered since ~2011)

validatedBenefits.citation: Schneider-Helmert & Schoenenberger, Eur Neurol, 1983
validatedBenefits.notes: First human study (6 volunteers, double-blind crossover): IV DSIP (25 nmol/kg) increased sleep by 59% in 130-min post-treatment window. Delayed effects included reduced stage 1 sleep and better sleep efficiency. However, the name 'delta sleep-inducing' is somewhat misleading as effects are modest.

validatedBenefits.citation: Schneider-Helmert & Schoenenberger, 1983
validatedBenefits.notes: Described as increasing 'pressure to sleep' rather than acting as a sedative. Subjects reported feeling of sleep pressure but were not forcibly sedated.

validatedBenefits.citation: Gaillard & Schoenenberger, Clin Endocrinol, 1995
validatedBenefits.notes: A study specifically tested this and found DSIP does NOT affect CRH- and meal-induced ACTH and cortisol secretion. This claim is NOT supported by the evidence.

validatedBenefits.citation: Kastin et al., Pharmacol Biochem Behav, 1984 (review)
validatedBenefits.notes: Analgesic properties noted in review literature, primarily from animal data. Limited human evidence for this specific claim.
No direct evidence found for enhanced recovery during sleep. This claim appears extrapolated from the sleep-promoting effects.

validatedBenefits.citation: Bes et al., Neuropsychobiology, 1992
validatedBenefits.notes: The double-blind study of 16 chronic insomniacs found ONLY weak, statistically borderline improvements in sleep efficiency and latency. Authors explicitly concluded 'short-term treatment of chronic insomnia with DSIP is not likely to be of major therapeutic benefit.' This claim overstates the evidence.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Reported in multiple clinical summaries. Logical given sleep-promoting mechanism.
Standard for subcutaneous peptide injections.
Reported anecdotally but consistent with mechanism.
Rarely reported in clinical literature.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 25 nmol/kg IV (approximately 250 mcg for 70kg adult) in clinical studies; subcutaneous dosing not formally studied
dosingValidation.notes: Our range aligns with the clinical study dose converted to mcg, though the original studies used IV infusion, not subcutaneous injection. Subcutaneous dosing is extrapolated, not clinically validated.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Appropriate. Human studies exist from 1980s-90s but are small, old, and showed mixed/weak results. The largest insomnia trial found no major therapeutic benefit. Mechanism described but not translated to robust clinical outcomes.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved. WADA status (wadaBanned: false) should be reviewed - DSIP likely falls under S0 (Non-Approved Substances) as it has no regulatory approval anywhere.

keyCitations.title: Acute and delayed effects of DSIP (delta sleep-inducing peptide) on human sleep behavior
Effects of delta sleep-inducing peptide on sleep of chronic insomniac patients. A double-blind study
Delta-sleep-inducing peptide (DSIP): a review
Delta-sleep-inducing peptide does not affect CRH and meal-induced ACTH and cortisol secretion
Delta sleep-inducing peptide (review)

corrections: CRITICAL: 'Reduces stress and cortisol' is contradicted by published evidence (PMID: 7777652). A study specifically found DSIP does NOT affect ACTH/cortisol secretion. This claim should be removed or flagged.
'Normalizes disturbed sleep patterns' significantly overstates the evidence. The largest insomnia study concluded DSIP is 'not likely to be of major therapeutic benefit.' Should be reworded.
'Enhances recovery during sleep' has no direct evidence and should be removed or clearly labeled as speculative.
WADA status should be reviewed - DSIP likely falls under S0 (Non-Approved Substances).
missingInfo: The 1992 double-blind insomnia study finding of 'not likely to be of major therapeutic benefit' should be mentioned in evidenceNotes for balance
FDA immunogenicity warning for compounded DSIP products
Note that all clinical studies used IV infusion, not subcutaneous injection - the SC route is not formally validated

validatedBenefits.claim: Stimulates natural LH and FSH production
validatedBenefits.citation: George et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2011
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-demonstrated in multiple human studies. IV kisspeptin-10 significantly elevated LH at all tested doses. FSH increased at higher doses (1.0 and 3.0 nmol/kg). Strong evidence from RCTs.

validatedBenefits.citation: George et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2011
validatedBenefits.notes: Serum testosterone significantly increased after IV kisspeptin-10 (0.3 or 1.0 nmol/kg). Continuous infusion increases testosterone, LH pulse frequency, and pulse size.

No published clinical trial specifically tested kisspeptin as an HCG alternative during TRT. This is a theoretical/extrapolated claim based on its ability to stimulate LH/FSH. Plausible but unproven in that specific clinical context.

validatedBenefits.citation: Jayasena et al., J Clin Invest, 2014
validatedBenefits.notes: Clinical study of 53 subfertile women. Kisspeptin-54 triggered egg maturation with minimum effective dose of 6.4 nmol/kg. Acceptable rates of egg maturation (75-85% metaphase II oocytes) at higher doses. Potential to reduce OHSS risk vs hCG.

No published study directly testing this claim. Extrapolated from LH-stimulating effects. The very short half-life (28 min) of kisspeptin raises questions about sustained gonadotropic stimulation needed for this purpose.

validatedBenefits.citation: Comninos et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab, 2025
validatedBenefits.notes: Recent 2025 review confirms kisspeptin's role in neuroendocrine regulation of reproductive behavior. However, a separate 2025 study (PMID: 40036336) found kisspeptin administration stimulates reproductive hormones but does NOT affect anxiety in humans. Sexual arousal enhancement is supported by brain imaging studies.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Jayasena et al., J Clin Invest, 2014
validatedSideEffects.notes: Most frequently reported effect across all clinical trials. Short-lived and self-resolving.

validatedSideEffects.claim: Very well tolerated in human studies
validatedSideEffects.notes: Across all published human kisspeptin trials, no serious adverse events reported. No changes in blood pressure, heart rate, or renal function.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: IV bolus: 0.3-3.0 nmol/kg kisspeptin-10; SC: 6.4-12.8 nmol/kg kisspeptin-54 (IVF trigger); Intranasal route also being studied
dosingValidation.citation: George et al., 2011 (PMID: 21632807); Jayasena et al., 2014
dosingValidation.notes: Clinical studies used weight-based IV/SC dosing in nmol/kg, not fixed mcg doses. The 100-200 mcg, 2-3 times per week dosing appears to come from wellness clinic protocols rather than published research. The half-life is only 28 minutes, which raises questions about the 2-3 times/week protocol. A recent 2025 study also explored intranasal delivery.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Multiple well-designed human RCTs, published in high-impact journals (JCEM, JCI). Dose-dependent effects clearly demonstrated. IVF application is Phase 2 level. Master regulator role in HPG axis well-established. Evidence is stronger than 'moderate' suggests.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved, used in clinical research. TAK-448 (kisspeptin analogue) is in Phase 2 trials. WADA status (wadaBanned: false) appears correct - kisspeptin is not specifically listed on the WADA prohibited list, though athletes should verify.

keyCitations.title: Kisspeptin-10 Is a Potent Stimulator of LH and Increases Pulse Frequency in Men
Kisspeptin-54 triggers egg maturation in women undergoing in vitro fertilization
The Effects of Kisspeptin-10 on Reproductive Hormone Release Show Sexual Dimorphism in Humans
Neuroendocrine Regulation of Reproductive Behavior and Emotional Control by Kisspeptin
Kisspeptins Regulating Fertility: Potential Future Therapeutic Approach in Infertility Treatment
Intranasal kisspeptin administration rapidly stimulates gonadotropin release in humans

corrections: 'Maintains fertility during TRT (HCG alternative)' is not directly supported by published research. Should be labeled as theoretical/extrapolated.
'Prevents testicular atrophy during TRT' is similarly unproven. Should be labeled as theoretical.
Dosing of 100-200 mcg 2-3x/week does not match published clinical trial protocols which use weight-based nmol/kg dosing. Should note this is from wellness clinics, not published studies.
Evidence level should be moderate_high based on multiple well-designed human RCTs in high-impact journals.
The very short half-life (28 min) should be mentioned as a significant limitation for chronic/maintenance use.
missingInfo: Half-life of only 28 minutes is a critical pharmacological limitation that should be mentioned
Intranasal delivery route is being studied (2025 publication) and could be a significant development
Kisspeptin's role as a PCOS biomarker is an emerging area of research
IVF-specific benefit of potentially reducing OHSS risk compared to hCG triggers

validatedBenefits.citation: Heffernan et al., Endocrinology, 2001
validatedBenefits.notes: Demonstrated in obese mice and beta-3-AR knockout mice. AOD-9604 stimulated lipolysis and inhibited lipogenesis. Animal data is supportive.

validatedBenefits.citation: Heffernan et al., Endocrinology, 2001
validatedBenefits.notes: Shown in animal models. The dual mechanism (pro-lipolysis, anti-lipogenesis) was the basis for clinical development.

validatedBenefits.claim: Preserves muscle tissue during fat loss
validatedBenefits.notes: No published evidence specifically demonstrating muscle preservation with AOD-9604. This claim appears extrapolated from the fact that AOD-9604 does not affect IGF-1/growth, but that is not the same as actively preserving muscle.

validatedBenefits.citation: Phase II clinical trial data; Moré et al., JOFEM
validatedBenefits.notes: Clinical data shows AOD-9604 does not impair insulin sensitivity or worsen blood sugar regulation. The Phase II trial showed improvement in glucose tolerance profiles.

validatedBenefits.claim: No effect on blood sugar or growth
validatedBenefits.citation: Phase II clinical trial data
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-established that AOD-9604 does not stimulate IGF-1, does not affect blood glucose, and does not cause growth effects. This is the primary safety differentiator from full HGH.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Most frequently reported side effect across clinical trials.
Occasionally reported in clinical trials.
Mild GI symptoms reported in some participants.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: Oral: 1 mg/day showed best results in Phase II (weight-based dosing up to 30 mg tested); Injectable SC dosing is not from clinical trials
dosingValidation.notes: The Phase II trial used ORAL dosing (1-30 mg), not injectable. The 250-500 mcg injectable dose cited in our data comes from wellness clinic protocols, not published research. The most effective clinical dose was 1 mg oral daily. Injectable SC dosing at 250-500 mcg has no formal clinical validation.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Appropriate. Six clinical trials with ~900 participants, but the largest Phase IIb trial (536 subjects, 24 weeks) FAILED to show statistical significance. Development was terminated in 2007. The evidence level is correctly rated as low_moderate.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved, Phase IIb trial failed 2007, removed from Category 2 Sept 2024. WADA banned status is correct (true). AOD-9604 falls under WADA S0 category and WADA has explicitly confirmed it is prohibited.

keyCitations.title: The effects of human GH and its lipolytic fragment (AOD9604) on lipid metabolism following chronic treatment in obese mice and beta(3)-AR knock-out mice
The effect of AOD9604 on weight loss in obese adults: Results of a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study
Safety and Metabolism of AOD9604, a Novel Nutraceutical Ingredient for Improved Metabolic Health
Obesity Pharmacotherapy: Current Perspectives and Future Directions

corrections: 'Preserves muscle tissue during fat loss' has no published evidence. Should be removed or clearly labeled as unproven.
Dosing of 250-500 mcg SC daily is from wellness protocols, not published research. Clinical trials used oral dosing (1-30 mg). This discrepancy should be noted.
Description already correctly notes the Phase IIb trial failure, which is good and honest.
missingInfo: The Phase II 1mg oral dose was the most effective (2.8 kg vs 0.8 kg placebo over 12 weeks) - this specific data point is useful context
AOD-9604 has GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) status as a food ingredient in Australia/New Zealand, which is distinct from pharmaceutical use
A 2024 PCAC meeting presentation (FDA-2024-N-4777-0009) attempted to save AOD-9604's compounding status

validatedBenefits.claim: Exercise mimetic (mimics benefits of physical exercise)
validatedBenefits.citation: Reynolds et al., Nature Communications, 2021
validatedBenefits.notes: Published in Nature Communications. MOTS-c is exercise-induced (11.9-fold increase in skeletal muscle after exercise) and when administered exogenously, enhances physical capacity in aged mice. The 'exercise mimetic' label is from the published literature.

validatedBenefits.claim: Stimulates glucose utilization and fat oxidation
validatedBenefits.citation: Lee et al., Cell Metabolism, 2015
validatedBenefits.notes: Original discovery paper. MOTS-c promotes metabolic homeostasis, reduces obesity and insulin resistance in mice. Mechanism via AMPK activation and inhibition of folate cycle/purine biosynthesis.

validatedBenefits.citation: Lee et al., Cell Metabolism, 2015; Kim et al., Physiol Reports, 2019
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-established in mouse models. MOTS-c prevented age-dependent and high-fat-diet-induced insulin resistance. Primary mechanism is AMPK activation in skeletal muscle. Called an 'equal opportunity insulin sensitizer' (PMC6462348).

validatedBenefits.citation: Zhai et al., Front Endocrinol, 2023
validatedBenefits.notes: Review article documents anti-inflammatory properties. Primarily from animal and in vitro data.

validatedBenefits.citation: Reynolds et al., Nature Communications, 2021
validatedBenefits.notes: Demonstrated in aged mice. MOTS-c treatment improved physical capacity and muscle homeostasis. No human exercise capacity trials.

validatedBenefits.citation: Zhai et al., Front Endocrinol, 2023
validatedBenefits.notes: Documented in review literature. MOTS-c effects related to stress, metabolism, and aging. Primarily animal/cell data.

validatedSideEffects.notes: CANNOT be confirmed. There are NO completed clinical trials in humans. USADA explicitly states 'it is unknown under what conditions (if any) it is safe to use MOTS-c.' Anecdotal reports include heart palpitations, insomnia, and fever.
Commonly reported in anecdotal user reports, standard for SC peptide injection.
Reported anecdotally. No clinical trial confirmation.
Reported anecdotally. Consistent with metabolic effects on skeletal muscle.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: No established human dosing. Animal studies: 0.5-15 mg/kg (weight-based, not fixed dose). Human dosing protocols are entirely from wellness clinics.
dosingValidation.citation: No clinical trial human dosing data exists
dosingValidation.notes: CRITICAL: There are no completed human clinical trials for MOTS-c. All human dosing is extrapolated from animal studies and wellness clinic protocols. Our stated dosing has no clinical validation.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: While the mechanistic data is published in high-impact journals (Cell Metabolism, Nature Communications), there are NO completed human clinical trials. All therapeutic claims are from animal/cell data. 'Moderate' overstates the clinical evidence. The correlation studies in humans (serum MOTS-c levels vs muscle strength) are observational, not interventional.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved. WADA banned status is correct (true) - MOTS-c was explicitly added to the 2024 WADA Prohibited List under S4 (Metabolic Modulators) as an AMPK activator.

keyCitations.title: The mitochondrial-derived peptide MOTS-c promotes metabolic homeostasis and reduces obesity and insulin resistance
MOTS-c is an exercise-induced mitochondrial-encoded regulator of age-dependent physical decline and muscle homeostasis
MOTS-c: A promising mitochondrial-derived peptide for therapeutic exploitation
The mitochondrial-derived peptide MOTS-c is a regulator of plasma metabolites and enhances insulin sensitivity
Mitochondria-derived peptide MOTS-c restores mitochondrial respiration in type 2 diabetic heart

corrections: CRITICAL: Evidence level should be low_moderate, not moderate. There are NO completed human clinical trials for MOTS-c.
'Generally well tolerated' side effect claim cannot be verified - no clinical trial safety data exists. USADA warns safety is unknown.
EvidenceNotes cite 'Nature Communications (2020)' - the actual publication date is January 2021 (PMID: 33441542). Should be corrected.
Dosing has no clinical validation and should be clearly noted as extrapolated from animal data.
Missing serious anecdotal side effects: heart palpitations, insomnia, and fever have been reported by users.
missingInfo: USADA explicitly warns 'it is unknown under what conditions (if any) it is safe to use MOTS-c' - this should be prominently noted
MOTS-c was only discovered in 2015, making it a very new research compound
The primary site of action is skeletal muscle (vs metformin which acts on liver)
Endogenous MOTS-c increases 11.9-fold in skeletal muscle and 1.6-fold in circulation during exercise

validatedBenefits.citation: Szeto et al., IJMS, 2025; TAZPOWER trial
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-established mechanism. Elamipretide binds cardiolipin on the inner mitochondrial membrane, stabilizing cristae structure and enhancing ATP production. Confirmed in multiple preclinical and clinical studies.

validatedBenefits.citation: Whitson et al., eLife, 2020; Campbell et al., PMC, 2024
validatedBenefits.notes: Mouse studies show late-life elamipretide treatment reverses cardiac dysfunction and improves skeletal muscle function. 2024 study confirmed improvement in cardiac and skeletal muscle function during aging. No human aging trials yet.

validatedBenefits.claim: Increases muscle mass and fatigue resistance
validatedBenefits.citation: TAZPOWER trial (Barth syndrome); Campbell et al., 2024
validatedBenefits.notes: TAZPOWER trial showed 45% improvement in knee extensor muscle strength in Barth syndrome patients. Mouse aging studies showed improved fatigue resistance. FDA approval was based on muscle strength improvement.

validatedBenefits.citation: Szeto et al., IJMS, 2025
validatedBenefits.notes: Comprehensive review confirms elamipretide reduces reactive oxygen species by stabilizing mitochondrial electron transport chain. Demonstrated across cardiac, renal, and skeletal muscle tissues.

validatedBenefits.citation: Butler et al., JACC Heart Fail, 2020; Sabbah et al., Circ Heart Fail, 2017
validatedBenefits.notes: PROGRESS-HF Phase 2 trial showed decreased LV volumes at highest dose. However, the primary endpoint (LVESV change at week 4) was NOT met. Cardioprotective potential exists but clinical benefit for heart failure is inconclusive.

validatedBenefits.citation: Stealth BioTherapeutics Phase II data
validatedBenefits.notes: Renal study showed elamipretide protected against transient hypoxia developing in placebo group. Neurological support primarily from animal data and the general mitochondrial mechanism.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Most common adverse reaction per FDA label. Can be treated with oral antihistamines or topical corticosteroids.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Multiple Phase II trials; FDA review
validatedSideEffects.notes: Confirmed across multiple clinical trials lasting up to 24 weeks. The FDA advisory committee voted 10-6 in favor of approval, validating acceptable safety profile.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: 40 mg SC once daily (FDA-approved dose for Barth syndrome); IV: 0.01-0.25 mg/kg/h over 4 hours (clinical trial dose-escalation)
dosingValidation.notes: Our range of 5-40 mg/day encompasses the FDA-approved dose (40 mg/day). The FDA-approved dose is specifically 40 mg SC daily for patients >=30 kg. The lower end (5 mg) reflects earlier dose-escalation studies.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Appropriate. FDA-approved (accelerated approval) for Barth syndrome with full clinical trial package. Multiple Phase II trials for other indications. 8+ years of follow-up data from original trial participants. The PROGRESS-HF trial for heart failure did NOT meet primary endpoint, but overall evidence level is high given FDA approval.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states FDA-approved for Barth syndrome (Sept 2025). Brand name is FORZINITY (elamipretide HCl). Available by prescription for Barth syndrome. Off-label use from research suppliers exists. WADA status should be noted: elamipretide likely falls under S4 (Metabolic Modulators) or S0 (Non-Approved Substances for off-label uses).

keyCitations.title: Elamipretide: A Review of Its Structure, Mechanism of Action, and Therapeutic Potential
Effects of Elamipretide on Left Ventricular Function in Patients With Heart Failure With Reduced Ejection Fraction: The PROGRESS-HF Phase 2 Trial
Novel Mitochondria-Targeting Peptide in Heart Failure Treatment: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Elamipretide
Randomized dose-escalation trial of elamipretide in adults with primary mitochondrial myopathy
Late-life restoration of mitochondrial function reverses cardiac dysfunction in old mice
The mitochondrial-targeted peptide therapeutic elamipretide improves cardiac and skeletal muscle function during aging
FDA Prescribing Information: FORZINITY (elamipretide) injection

corrections: PROGRESS-HF heart failure trial did NOT meet its primary endpoint (LVESV change). Cardioprotective claims should note this limitation.
WADA status should be clarified - elamipretide is likely prohibited under S4 or S0 for athletes.
Brand name FORZINITY should be added to aliases
missingInfo: Brand name FORZINITY should be in aliases list
The FDA advisory committee voted 10-6 in favor - close vote indicating some debate about evidence
More than half of original TAZPOWER trial patients continue treatment 8+ years later
Dosage modification required for adults with renal impairment per FDA label
The MMPOWER-3 trial for primary mitochondrial myopathy did NOT meet its primary endpoint

validatedBenefits.claim: Induces tanning within ~5 doses without UV
validatedBenefits.citation: DermNet NZ; Phase I trial data
validatedBenefits.notes: Melanotan II stimulates melanocyte activity and melanin production. Tanning effect is well-documented. However, UV exposure typically enhances the effect.

validatedBenefits.citation: Phase I clinical trial data
validatedBenefits.notes: Spontaneous erections reported in males 1-5 hours after administration. This is a well-documented effect due to melanocortin receptor activation. PT-141 (bremelanotide) was derived from Melanotan II specifically for this purpose.

validatedBenefits.claim: May provide some UV protection via melanin
validatedBenefits.citation: Theoretical/mechanistic
validatedBenefits.notes: Increased melanin does provide some degree of UV protection. However, this should NOT be interpreted as cancer protection, as melanocyte stimulation itself carries risks.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Nausea reported at doses as low as 0.025 mg/kg in Phase I trial. Very common side effect.
Warmth and redness typically lasting 1-2 hours. Well-documented.
Deepening of existing moles, new moles, and atypical melanocytic naevi are well-documented effects.

At least 5 case reports of melanomas during or after Melanotan II use. A 2025 case report described oral mucosal malignant melanoma potentially linked to Melanotan II nasal spray. While direct causation is not proven, melanocyte stimulation raises significant concern. Development was abandoned partly due to safety concerns.

Case report of renal infarction attributed to Melanotan II. Additionally, rhabdomyolysis and renal dysfunction have been documented (PMID: 23121206). Thrombotic mechanism and direct toxic effects on renal parenchyma considered.
Reported in clinical literature but rare. The non-selective melanocortin agonism contributes to widespread systemic effects.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: Phase I trial: 0.025 mg/kg (nausea threshold); No approved therapeutic dosing exists; Underground protocols: 250-500 mcg loading, then maintenance
dosingValidation.citation: Phase I trial data; DermNet NZ
dosingValidation.notes: The stated dosing matches commonly used underground protocols. There is no FDA-approved dosing. Phase I trial dosing was 0.025 mg/kg, which is approximately 1.75 mg for a 70kg adult - higher than the typical underground dose.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Appropriate. Phase I trial data exists. Multiple case reports document both efficacy and serious adverse events. Development was abandoned due to safety concerns, not lack of efficacy. The tanning and libido effects are well-established.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved and illegal to purchase in US and UK. The nuance is that it is not a DEA-controlled substance but is an unapproved drug whose sale for human consumption violates FDA regulations. Vendors can sell 'for research purposes only.' UK bans medicinal products containing MT-II. WADA banned status (true) is correct. TGA in Australia also warns against use.

keyCitations.title: Melanoma associated with the use of melanotan-II
Melanotan II: a possible cause of renal infarction: review of the literature and case report
Melanotan II injection resulting in systemic toxicity and rhabdomyolysis
Melanotan II nasal spray: a possible risk factor for oral mucosal malignant melanoma?
An overview of benefits and risks of chronic melanocortin-1 receptor activation

corrections: No major factual errors found. The description and risk assessment are appropriately severe.
Minor: could add priapism (prolonged erection) as a known side effect in males, reported in Phase I trial
Could note that development was abandoned due to SAFETY concerns specifically, not efficacy
missingInfo: Priapism (prolonged erections) is a documented side effect in males from Phase I trial
Spontaneous erections with yawning/stretching complex (1-5 hours post-dose) should be noted
TGA (Australia) warning against use should be mentioned alongside US/UK bans
2025 case report of oral mucosal malignant melanoma linked to MT-II nasal spray is important new safety data
Rhabdomyolysis case report (PMID: 23121206) should be added to serious side effects

validatedBenefits.citation: Rodino-Klapac et al., Mol Ther, 2009; Kota et al., Hum Gene Ther, 2009
validatedBenefits.notes: Well-established mechanism. Follistatin is a potent myostatin antagonist. Also antagonizes activin. This is the core mechanism supported by extensive preclinical data.

validatedBenefits.claim: Promotes muscle growth through multiple pathways
validatedBenefits.citation: Lee, PLoS ONE, 2007 (animal data)
validatedBenefits.notes: Follistatin increases muscle growth even in myostatin-knockout mice, demonstrating multiple pathways beyond myostatin inhibition alone. Primarily animal and gene therapy data.

validatedBenefits.citation: Kota et al., Hum Gene Ther, 2009
validatedBenefits.notes: AAV1-FS344 in macaque monkeys induced pronounced and durable increases in muscle size and strength. Long-term expression of the transgene without abnormal changes in organ morphology or function. Gene therapy approach, not peptide injection.

validatedBenefits.citation: Mendell et al., Mol Ther, 2015; Al-Zaidy et al., J Neuromuscul Dis, 2015
validatedBenefits.notes: Phase 1/2a gene therapy trial in 6 Becker Muscular Dystrophy patients. Patients improved 58-125 meters on 6-minute walk test. Fibrosis reduced to 35-43% of baseline. This is gene therapy, NOT injectable peptide.

validatedSideEffects.notes: Standard for SC injections. Note: clinical trials used gene therapy (AAV vector), not SC peptide injection.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Only 6 patients in Phase 1/2a gene therapy trial
validatedSideEffects.notes: Correctly identifies the critical gap. Only human data is from gene therapy in 6 BMD patients, not exogenous peptide injection.

validatedSideEffects.citation: Mechanistic concern; follistatin modulates activin in HPG axis
validatedSideEffects.notes: FS-344 produces both systemic (FS-315, myostatin binding) and gonadal (FS-288, activin binding) effects. Cannot selectively get myostatin inhibition without activin modulation. FSH suppression is a theoretical concern. Kota 2009 primate study found no permanent reproductive effects.
Theoretical concern: rapid muscle growth without proportional tendon/ligament strengthening. No published evidence directly confirming this.

dosingValidation.publishedRange: No published human dosing data for SC peptide injection. Gene therapy: direct intramuscular AAV1.CMV.FS344 injection. Underground protocols: 100-200 mcg, never exceed 200 mcg/day.
dosingValidation.citation: No clinical trial data for SC peptide dosing
dosingValidation.notes: CRITICAL: There is NO formal human study on exogenous SC peptide injection dosing. All clinical evidence is from gene therapy (AAV vector delivery). The 100-200 mcg dose comes entirely from underground/wellness protocols. Also: 17 supplements marketed as containing follistatin were tested - only 9 actually contained it.

evidenceLevelValidation.reasoning: Correctly rated. While gene therapy data is strong (Phase 1/2a trial in BMD), there is ZERO published human research on exogenous peptide injection. The gap between gene therapy evidence and injectable peptide use is enormous.

legalStatusUpdate.notes: Correctly states not FDA-approved. WADA banned (true) is correct - Follistatin 344 has been on the WADA Prohibited List since 2019, classified under S4.3 (Metabolic Modulators).

keyCitations.title: A Phase 1/2a Follistatin Gene Therapy Trial for Becker Muscular Dystrophy
Follistatin Gene Therapy Improves Ambulation in Becker Muscular Dystrophy
Follistatin Gene Delivery Enhances Muscle Growth and Strength in Nonhuman Primates
Inhibition of myostatin with emphasis on follistatin as a therapy for muscle disease
Detection of black market follistatin 344

corrections: Dosing description should prominently note that NO human clinical data exists for SC peptide injection. All dosing is from underground protocols.
The existing notes correctly state 'No formal human studies on SC peptide injection. Most evidence from gene therapy.' - this is accurate and should remain.
Minor: LDL cholesterol increase (~8 mg/dl in ~1/3 of patients) reported in gene therapy should be noted as a potential metabolic effect
missingInfo: The BMD gene therapy trial results (58-125 meter improvement on 6MWT, fibrosis reduced to 35-43% baseline) are important context
17 follistatin supplements tested, only 9 contained actual follistatin (product quality concern from PMID: 31758732)
FS-344 undergoes post-translational modification to FS-315 (serum-based) which has 10-fold lower activin affinity than FS-288 - this is relevant to the reproductive effects discussion
WADA ban since 2019 under S4.3 should be specified in legal notes
Potential LDL cholesterol increase should be added to side effects

benefitClaimsContradictedByEvidence: DSIP 'Reduces stress and cortisol' - directly contradicted by PMID 7777652 showing DSIP does NOT affect ACTH/cortisol
DSIP 'Normalizes disturbed sleep patterns' - largest insomnia study concluded NOT likely of major therapeutic benefit
Kisspeptin 'Maintains fertility during TRT' - no published clinical trial supports this specific use case
AOD-9604 'Preserves muscle tissue during fat loss' - no published evidence
evidenceLevelCorrections: MOTS-c: moderate -> low_moderate (no completed human clinical trials)
Kisspeptin: moderate -> moderate_high (multiple well-designed human RCTs in high-impact journals)
dosingConcerns: MOTS-c: ALL human dosing is extrapolated from animal studies, no clinical validation
Follistatin 344: ALL human dosing is from underground protocols, zero clinical data for SC injection
AOD-9604: Clinical trials used oral dosing (1-30 mg), not injectable SC (250-500 mcg)
Kisspeptin: Clinical trials use weight-based nmol/kg dosing, not fixed mcg doses
Selank: Our range (250-500 mcg/day) is below most published protocols (750-1000 mcg/day)
wadaStatusCorrections: Selank (wadaBanned: false) - effectively banned under S0 Non-Approved Substances
Semax (wadaBanned: false) - effectively banned under S0 Non-Approved Substances
DSIP (wadaBanned: false) - likely banned under S0 Non-Approved Substances
newSafetySignals: Melanotan II: 2025 case report of oral mucosal malignant melanoma linked to nasal spray use
MOTS-c: USADA explicitly warns safety is unknown, anecdotal reports of heart palpitations and insomnia
SS-31: PROGRESS-HF and MMPOWER-3 trials did NOT meet primary endpoints despite FDA approval for Barth syndrome
